text: The surgeon sweeps the omentum upward, revealing the uterus and the right ovary. The small intestine is pushed above the sacral promontory to maintain a clear view. The iliac vessels, cecum, and the tip of the appendix are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of dome, with the infundibulopelvic ligament visible. The ureter is seen deep in the field, with potential peristalsis observable. The left fimbrial end of the tube and a normal ovary are visible, with a distended sigmoid colon in the foreground.

The utero-sacral ligament and cul-de-sac are visible, located approximately 2.5 centimeters lateral to the utero-sacral ligament. The ureter is seen crossing near the ovarian fossa. The interior and posterior cul-de-sac are clear, with no signs of endometriosis, PID, or free fluid. The ovary and tube are visible, and the sigmoid colon is retracted to the left, revealing the sacral promontory.

The camera pans over the peritoneum, revealing the ascending colon, liver, gallbladder, and a few drops of trickled blood. The left lobe of the liver, stomach, diaphragm, and omentum covering the spleen are visible before the view returns to the pelvis.
During the diagnostic laparoscopy, methylene blue dye is injected, visibly dribbling from both the left and right fallopian tubes, indicating tubal patency. The dye is then washed away with saline.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to remove remaining methylene blue dye from the pelvic cavity, following the completion of the diagnostic laparoscopy and tubal potency test. The uterus, tubes, and ovaries appear normal with no visible pathology. The laparoscopic telescope is removed, and the procedure concludes without the need for closure of the port wounds.
